question_title: Will Tadej Pogačar win Stage 19 (Gap → Alpe d'Huez) of the 2026 Tour de France?
summary: Pogačar is historically dominant in this 2026 Tour (yellow +2:42, 2 stage wins by Stage 9, peak form), which supports a stage win. But three structural forces cap the probability: (1) Alpe d'Huez heavily favors breakaways (4 of 5 recent road-stage finishes), (2) Stage 19 is the FIRST of two back-to-back Alpe d'Huez finishes with the harder Galibier queen stage on Stage 20, creating a strong incentive to let a break win the shorter Stage 19, and (3) even in a GC-decided scenario, rivals like Vingegaard, del Toro, and Ayuso can win. A probability-tree decomposition (starts healthy 0.90 × [GC decides 0.42 × Pogačar wins among GC 0.62 + break-but-Pogačar-overpowers 0.10]) yields ~0.29, with sensitivity range 0.23–0.36. Nudged to 0.30 for Pogačar's exceptional form and appetite for a prestigious climb he has never won.

factors.description: Pogačar is the dominant rider of the race, in yellow +2:42, 2 stage wins by Stage 9, at peak form and hunts stage wins aggressively even when GC is secure
Alpe d'Huez historically favors breakaways (4 of 5 recent road-stage finishes won by breaks; yellow-jersey wins very rare)
First of back-to-back Alpe d'Huez finishes with the harder Galibier queen stage next day, strong incentive to let a break win Stage 19
Even in a GC-decided scenario, other elite climbers (Vingegaard, del Toro, Ayuso) can take the stage win
Crash/illness/abandonment risk over 10 intervening stages including mountains

reasoning:
## Forecast: 30% probability

**Will Tadej Pogačar win Stage 19 (Gap → Alpe d'Huez) of the 2026 Tour de France?**

My estimate is **0.30 (YES)**.

**Reasoning:**
- **For:** Pogačar is dominating the 2026 Tour (yellow jersey +2:42, 2 stage wins by Stage 9, peak form) and aggressively hunts stage wins, especially prestigious ones like Alpe d'Huez, which he has never won.
- **Against:** Three structural forces cap the probability:
  1. Alpe d'Huez heavily favors breakaways (4 of 5 recent road-stage finishes went to breaks; yellow-jersey wins are rare)
  2. Stage 19 is the *first* of two back-to-back Alpe d'Huez finishes, with the harder Galibier queen stage the next day — a strong incentive to concede the shorter Stage 19 to a break
  3. Even in a GC-decided finish, rivals (Vingegaard, del Toro, Ayuso) can take the win

A probability-tree decomposition yields ~0.29 (sensitivity range 0.23–0.36), nudged to **0.30** for Pogačar's exceptional current form. Even for the sport's most dominant rider, a single specific mountain stage win on a breakaway-favoring climb rarely exceeds ~35%.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if Tadej Pogačar is listed as the winner (rank 1) of Stage 19 of the 2026 Tour de France, the 127.9 km stage from Gap to Alpe d'Huez scheduled for Friday, July 24, 2026, in the official stage classification published at https://www.letour.fr/en/rankings (select Stage 19). It resolves NO if any other rider is listed as the winner. If the stage is cancelled, shortened, or its winner is otherwise not designated by the race organiser, or if Pogačar does not start the stage, the question resolves NO.
fine_print: If the official winner is later changed due to a disqualification or appeal, the final classification as recorded by the race organiser (ASO) shall govern. If letour.fr is unavailable, the result may be confirmed via [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions).

factors.description: Pogačar is the dominant rider of the race, in yellow +2:42, 2 stage wins by Stage 9, clearly at peak form and hunts stage wins aggressively
Alpe d'Huez historically favors breakaways (4 of 5 recent road-stage finishes won by breaks; yellow-jersey wins very rare)
First of back-to-back Alpe d'Huez finishes with harder queen stage (Galibier) next day → strong incentive to let a break win Stage 19
Even in GC-decided scenario, other elite climbers (Vingegaard, del Toro, Ayuso) can take the stage
Crash/illness/abandonment risk over 10 intervening stages incl. mountains

input.anchor: Base rate for a dominant favorite winning a specific mountain summit finish ~30-35%, discounted by Alpe d'Huez breakaway tendency
input.assessment: Probability-tree decomposition: P(starts healthy ~0.90) × [P(GC group decides stage ~0.42) × P(Pogačar wins among GC ~0.62) + P(break wins but Pogačar overpowers ~0.10)] ≈ 0.29. Sensitivity range 0.23–0.36. The dominant downward force is Alpe d'Huez's strong breakaway tendency amplified by the unique double-header (Stage 19 is the 'lesser' of two identical finishes, so teams likely conserve for the Galibier queen stage on Stage 20). Countered by Pogačar's exceptional current dominance and appetite for prestigious stage wins on a climb he's never won. A single specific stage win for even a dominant rider rarely exceeds ~35-40%.
input.key_uncertainties: Whether UAE/Pogačar contest Stage 19 or concede it to a break to save for Stage 20 queen stage; GC gap size by Stage 19 (larger gap = more likely to let break go)
input.tool_audit: research() returned excellent structured data on current GC standings and Alpe d'Huez historical base rates. sandbox used for probability tree. No failures.
input.process_reflection: Smooth. The research sub-agent gave directly usable base rates (breakaway vs GC win split at Alpe d'Huez) and live race state, which is exactly what a single-stage cycling forecast needs. No friction.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
All five factors are well-grounded in the research trace with no hallucination. Verified against tool results: Pogačar in yellow at +2:42 over Vingegaard with 2 stage wins by Stage 9 (Factor 1); Alpe d'Huez 4-of-5 recent breakaway base rate — Rolland/Riblon/Pinot/Thomas/Pidcock, with only Thomas 2018 winning in yellow (Factor 2); the unprecedented back-to-back Alpe d'Huez double-header with the Galibier/Sarenne queen stage on Stage 20 creating a conservation incentive (Factor 3); elite climbing rivals del Toro/Ayuso/Vingegaard present (Factor 4); and Pogačar's 0 career Alpe d'Huez wins. All confirmed in the trace.

No double-counting: Factors 2 and 3 both push negative but rest on distinct evidence (general historical base rate vs. the 2026-specific double-header tactical wrinkle). Factor signs are all directionally correct (Factor 1 positive favoring Pogačar, 2–5 negative). No pre-publication event issue — Stage 19 is July 24, after the July 13 publication, so the forecast is properly forward-looking. Resolution criteria are provided and the factors engage with the specific terms (rank-1 winner; the health/abandonment factor covers the "does not start → NO" clause). The tool_audit claim of "no failures" matches ground truth (0/54 errors).

Adversarial self-examination is strong, not a token gesture: the counterargument constructs a coherent alternative (a historically dominant Pogačar with no GC reason to conserve winning both Alpe stages, implying a 45-50% summit-finish win rate) and specifies concrete falsifiers. Confidence of 0.70 is well-calibrated given the genuine tactical uncertainty (whether UAE chases or lets a break go).

On the probability: 29% sits at the low end of a defensible range. There's a reasonable case that Pogačar's historic dominance and status as the best climber in the world on a pure summit finish support something closer to 32-38%, and the agent's own counterargument acknowledges this. But the strong 80% breakaway base rate and the double-header conservation dynamic are legitimate, well-sourced counterweights, and the probability tree (centered 0.29, range 0.23-0.36) is coherent. My independent estimate would land around 30-34% — close enough to the agent's 29% that it is not meaningfully wrong. No fail-level concern. Worldview research entries are the agent's own and contain no contradictions. Approve.
